content.text: MCP's value proposition is that any server works with any host. A tool server you build for Claude Desktop today works in VS Code, Cursor, and your own custom agents without modification — the protocol is the adapter. This section covers the major MCP-capable host surfaces, how to configure servers for each, and patterns for making your servers work well across all of them.

attrs.alt: MCP integration landscape showing five host types (Claude Desktop, Claude Code, VS Code, Cursor, custom agents) connecting through JSON-RPC 2.0 to any MCP server (GitHub, databases, productivity, web, cloud, custom)

content.text: Build once, use everywhere. A server exposes its tools via the MCP protocol; which host drives it is irrelevant to the server. The server never imports or references the host SDK — it only speaks JSON-RPC 2.0.
Claude Desktop reads its server configuration from
in the platform's application support directory:

Claude Desktop spawns each server as a subprocess at startup and keeps them running for the session. Servers appear under a wrench icon in the UI; you can inspect which tools are available from each server.
Claude Code (the CLI) reads servers from
in the project root or from the global user config. You can manage servers interactively with the

# Add a server globally (available in all projects)
claude mcp add github-server \
  -e GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN=ghp_... \
  -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-github

# Add a server scoped to the current project
claude mcp add local-db \
  -s project \
  -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://localhost/mydb \
  -- uvx mcp-server-postgres

# List configured servers
claude mcp list

# Remove a server
claude mcp remove github-server
and should be committed to the repository so all team members get the same tool setup. Global servers are in

in the workspace for team-scoped servers, or to user settings for personal tools:
VS Code also supports HTTP+SSE servers (type
Cursor supports MCP servers in agent mode. Configure them in

syntax forwards the variable from the shell environment. This keeps credentials out of the config file.
For agents you build directly with the Anthropic API or other LLM SDKs, connect to MCP servers programmatically using
. The agent itself is the host:

import asyncio, os
import anthropic
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def run_agent(task: str):
    params = StdioServerParameters(
        command="npx",
        args=["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
        env={"GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN": os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]},
    )
    async with stdio_client(params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools_resp = await session.list_tools()

            # Convert MCP tools to Anthropic API format
            tool_defs = [
                {"name": t.name, "description": t.description, "input_schema": t.inputSchema}
                for t in tools_resp.tools
            ]

            client = anthropic.Anthropic()
            messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]

            while True:
                resp = client.messages.create(
                    model="claude-sonnet-5", max_tokens=4096,
                    tools=tool_defs, messages=messages,
                )
                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})

                tool_uses = [b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
                if not tool_uses:
                    return next(b.text for b in resp.content if hasattr(b, "text"))

                results = []
                for tu in tool_uses:
                    result = await session.call_tool(tu.name, dict(tu.input))
                    results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": tu.id,
                        "content": result.content[0].text if result.content else "",
                        "is_error": result.isError,
                    })
                messages.append({"role": "user", "content": results})

answer = asyncio.run(run_agent("What are the 5 most recently opened issues in anthropics/anthropic-sdk-python?"))
print(answer)
Each host uses a slightly different config file path and key name, but the server specification is identical:
Writing Servers That Work Well Across Hosts
To maximize compatibility across the major hosts:
Keep tool names short and unambiguous.
Hosts may truncate long names in their UI. Avoid spaces — use underscores (
The description is what the LLM reads to decide which tool to call. One sentence stating the action and its primary parameter is enough.
Use environment variables for all credentials.
Every host passes env vars to the subprocess. Hardcoded credentials in the config file prevent sharing.
stdout carries the JSON-RPC stream; any non-JSON output there breaks every host.
Hosts display tool errors in their UI. A clear error message lets the user or model take corrective action.

The cleanest way to share MCP server configuration across a team is to commit the project-scoped config file. Each developer installs the required packages (via npm/pip) and sets their own credentials as environment variables.

# Setup script for new team members (onboarding.sh)
#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Install MCP servers globally so npx doesn't re-download them each time
npm install -g \
  @modelcontextprotocol/server-github@2.3.1 \
  @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem@2.1.0

pip install mcp-server-postgres==0.4.2

# Set credentials in shell profile (developers fill in their own values)
echo 'export GITHUB_TOKEN=""' >> ~/.zshrc
echo 'export DATABASE_URL=""' >> ~/.zshrc

echo "MCP servers installed. Fill in credentials in ~/.zshrc."
— each developer's shell environment provides the actual values. No credentials are ever committed.
When your MCP server runs as an HTTP service (useful for shared team infrastructure or SaaS integrations), hosts connect via the SSE transport. The config specifies a URL instead of a command:
Running a shared HTTP+SSE server has several advantages for team deployments:
— API keys live on the server; developers only need an
— the server runs in a container; developers just add a URL to their config.
request with the user identity from the auth token.
— update the server once and all clients pick up the new tool definitions immediately.

# Minimal HTTP+SSE server with per-request auth validation
import asyncio, os
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from starlette.routing import Route
from starlette.responses import Response
import uvicorn
from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.server.sse import SseServerTransport
from mcp.types import Tool, TextContent

VALID_TOKENS = set(os.environ.get("VALID_TOKENS", "").split(","))

app = Server("company-search")

@app.list_tools()
async def list_tools():
    return [
        Tool(
            name="search_docs",
            description="Search internal documentation",
            inputSchema={"type":"object","properties":{"query":{"type":"string"}},"required":["query"]},
        )
    ]

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "search_docs":
        # Real implementation queries your internal search API
        return [TextContent(type="text", text=f"Results for: {arguments['query']}")]
    raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {name}")

sse = SseServerTransport("/message")

async def handle_sse(request):
    # Validate auth before opening SSE stream
    auth = request.headers.get("Authorization", "")
    token = auth.removeprefix("Bearer ").strip()
    if token not in VALID_TOKENS:
        return Response("Unauthorized", status_code=401)

    async with sse.connect_sse(request.scope, request.receive, request._send) as (r, w):
        await app.run(r, w, app.create_initialization_options())

starlette_app = Starlette(routes=[
    Route("/sse", endpoint=handle_sse),
    Route("/message", endpoint=sse.handle_post_message, methods=["POST"]),
])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(starlette_app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
Before declaring a server production-ready, verify it works in each host your team uses. The minimal compatibility test list:
in a Python script to call every tool and confirm correct output.
, and ask it to use one of your tools by name.
— intentionally pass invalid arguments (missing required field, bad type) and confirm the server returns
with a clear message rather than crashing.
— change the env var and restart the server. Confirm it picks up the new value (no in-memory credential caching across restarts).

# compatibility_test.py — runs against any MCP server
import asyncio, sys
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def compatibility_test(params: StdioServerParameters):
    print(f"\nTesting: {params.command} {' '.join(params.args)}")
    passed = 0
    failed = 0

    async with stdio_client(params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            # Test 1: initialize
            try:
                init = await session.initialize()
                print(f"  ✓ initialize — server: {init.serverInfo.name}")
                passed += 1
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"  ✗ initialize — {e}")
                failed += 1
                return  # Can't continue

            # Test 2: list_tools returns at least one tool
            try:
                tools = await session.list_tools()
                assert len(tools.tools) > 0
                print(f"  ✓ list_tools — {len(tools.tools)} tool(s): {[t.name for t in tools.tools]}")
                passed += 1
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"  ✗ list_tools — {e}")
                failed += 1

    print(f"  Result: {passed} passed, {failed} failed")
    return failed == 0

params = StdioServerParameters(command=sys.argv[1], args=sys.argv[2:])
asyncio.run(compatibility_test(params))
# Usage: python compatibility_test.py npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-github
Claude Desktop, Claude Code, VS Code Copilot, Cursor, and custom agents all support MCP — any server works with any of them.
Config format differs by host (key name, file path) but the server spec (command, args, env) is identical.
to the repository so all team members share the same server setup.
to connect programmatically — no config file needed.
Design servers for portability: short tool names, clear descriptions, env-var credentials, stderr-only logging, and graceful error returns.
